1. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** Originally spelled **Wilfrith**.[[5]](#cite_note-Fraser47-5)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-49)** Annemund was murdered at the command of [Balthild](/wiki/Balthild "Balthild"), the regent of [Chlothar III](/wiki/Chlothar_III "Chlothar III").[[47]](#cite_note-Levinson10-48)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-68)** Agilbert was later expelled from his English bishopric by the King of Wessex when the king could not understand Agilbert.[[65]](#cite_note-Barbarian263-67)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-71)** It is unclear how much of the speech in Bede's account of the council is actually Wilfrid's and how much was composed by Bede.[[64]](#cite_note-Intro131-66)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-73)** Some Welsh churches did not adopt the Roman Easter until 768.[[68]](#cite_note-Conversion117-72)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-80)** York did not attain metropolitan status until 735.[[74]](#cite_note-Hall-79)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-96)** Theodore shortly afterwards reconsecrated Ceadda and gave him the [bishopric of Lichfield](/wiki/Bishop_of_Lichfield "Bishop of Lichfield").[[89]](#cite_note-Earliest90-95) The *Vita Sancti Wilfrithi* says that Wilfrid urged Theodore to appoint Ceadda to Lichfield.[[21]](#cite_note-DNB-22)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-107)** Dagobert became king of [Austrasia](/wiki/Austrasia "Austrasia") in 676, but was assassinated in 680.[[99]](#cite_note-Long238-106)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-131)** And he was the only English bishop to appeal a royal verdict to the papacy until 1088 when [William de St-Calais](/wiki/William_de_St-Calais "William de St-Calais") appealed a decision of King [William II of England](/wiki/William_II_of_England "William II of England") to Rome.[[122]](#cite_note-West184-130)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-137)** The copy of the decrees of Agatho has had interpolations added to it, partly to support the later [Canterbury–York disputes](/wiki/Canterbury%E2%80%93York_dispute "Canterbury–York dispute") over primacy that started under Archbishop [Lanfranc](/wiki/Lanfranc "Lanfranc") after the [Norman conquest of England](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_England "Norman conquest of England"). However, the *Life of Wilfrid* also confirms the basics of the council decrees, it is only in the decrees discussion of metropolitan status for Theodore that it is possibly corrupt.[[127]](#cite_note-Gibbs217-136)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-153)** When Wilfrid returned to Northumbria, he gave the Wight mission to his nephew,[[84]](#cite_note-Reassess34-90) Beornwine, who was not apparently an ordained priest.[[142]](#cite_note-Society90-152)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-159)** The only authority for the expulsion of Bosa is Stephen of Ripon's *Vita Sancti Wilfrithi*, and it is possible that Bosa was not expelled, as he was still bishop at his death in 706.[[147]](#cite_note-Usurp20-158)
13. **[^](#cite_ref-190)** The book, which was given to Ripon, does not survive.[[177]](#cite_note-World228-189)
14. **[^](#cite_ref-194)** Both years are given as death dates in sources. The discrepancy over his death date involves the fact that two dates were associated with Wilfrid's cult, 24 April and 12 October. Stephen of Ripon expressly states that Wilfrid died on a Thursday, and neither date in 709 was a Thursday. 24 April 710, however, was a Thursday, and is likely to be Wilfrid's death date. A complication is the fact that the October date is the more common commemoration date, but the April date is the one first associated with Wilfrid's cult, appearing in 7th- and 8th-century saints calendars. The October date probably arose because the April date conflicted with [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") and [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter").[[21]](#cite_note-DNB-22)
15. **[^](#cite_ref-195)** The epitaph is recorded in Book V, Chapter XIX. An online translation is at the Medieval Sourcebook, part of the Online Reference Book for Medieval Studies by the [City University of New York](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York "City University of New York").
16. **[^](#cite_ref-199)** The historian [Clare Stancliffe](/wiki/Clare_Stancliffe "Clare Stancliffe") believes the white arc was actually a [moonbow](/wiki/Moonbow "Moonbow") or "lunar rainbow". Using information on the years that a moonbow was most likely to occur she establishes Thursday 24 April 710 as the date of Wilfrid's death.[[183]](#cite_note-stancliffe21-198)
17. **[^](#cite_ref-212)** The early 12th century writer Eadmer, who wrote a *Vita Sancti Wilfrithi*, took this approach to the problem of the differing accounts of Wilfrid's relics, and appears to have been the first writer to suggest this solution.[[35]](#cite_note-Philpott101-36)
